chapter.name: What Accounting Is & A = L + E

doc.title: The accounting equation — and the first entry ever
doc.event: Maya opens a design studio by depositing $10,000 of her own money into a business bank account. Show what happens to A = L + E, and record the very first journal entry of the business's life.

variants.label: Every event moves the equation — and it always stays balanced

lines.label: an asset arrives
lines.why: The business now HAS something: $10,000 of cash. Assets are what the business owns or controls.
lines.trap: The business and Maya are separate — this is the business's cash now, not hers. That separation (the economic entity idea) is why the credit side exists at all.

lines.label: who has claim to it
lines.why: Every asset is claimed by someone: creditors (liabilities) or owners (equity). Maya's claim on the business = equity.
lines.trap: Not revenue! The business didn't EARN this — the owner contributed it. Contributions and earnings are different doors into equity.

computationPaths.narration: A = L + E is not a formula to solve — it's a statement that's ALWAYS true: everything the business has (A) is claimed by someone (L + E). Every transaction moves at least two things and leaves the equation balanced.

memorize.body: A = L + E. Assets = what the business has. Liabilities = creditors' claims. Equity = owners' claim (contributions + earnings kept). Always balanced, after every single transaction.
Two questions for any event: what does the business HAVE now (left side)? and WHO has claim to it (right side)? Answer both and you've done accounting.

questions.prompt: The owner deposits $10,000 into the new business's account. The credit is to…

distractors.feedback: Not revenue — the business earned nothing. Owner contributions credit Owner's Capital (equity). Revenue is the OTHER door into equity, opened only by serving customers.

build.scaffold: What arrived (debit it), and whose claim grew (credit it)? The two decoys are the two classic wrong answers: it's not earned, and it's not borrowed.
